metadata.title: TCS UI MCQ Verified Question Bank
metadata.targetExamContext: TCS Ninja / TCS iON style HTML-CSS-JS assessments
notes: Questions are rewritten and normalized from standards + prep-pattern sources.
Raw candidate file is retained before final verification export.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 1 - HTML Foundations
sets.description: Core HTML structure, semantics, and essential tags frequently seen in entry-level UI MCQs.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of <!DOCTYPE html> in an HTML file?
options: It provides alternative text for accessibility and failed image loads.
It tells the browser to use HTML5 standards mode.
It is used to control layout behavior.
questions.explanation: The doctype declaration enables standards-compliant rendering in modern browsers.

questions.question: Which tag represents the root element of a valid HTML document?
questions.explanation: All visible and metadata content must live inside the root <html> element.

questions.question: Which section typically contains metadata, links, and page-level configuration?
questions.explanation: The <head> section stores metadata, title, link, script references, and meta tags.

questions.question: Which tag defines the text shown on the browser tab?
questions.explanation: The <title> element inside <head> controls tab title and bookmark name.

questions.question: Which tag should be used for the most important heading on a page?
questions.explanation: Only one main heading is typically recommended for clear semantic structure.

questions.question: Which HTML element is used for a paragraph of text?
questions.explanation: The paragraph element groups related sentences as a text block.

questions.question: Which tag is used to create an unordered list?
questions.explanation: Unordered lists show items with bullets by default.

questions.question: Which tag is used to create an ordered (numbered) list?
questions.explanation: Ordered lists render items in sequence, usually numbered.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the alt attribute in an tag?
options: It provides alternative text for accessibility and failed image loads.
It tells the browser to use HTML5 standards mode.
It is used to control layout behavior.
questions.explanation: Screen readers and fallback rendering rely on alt text.

questions.question: Which attribute specifies the destination URL in an anchor element?
questions.explanation: Without href, an anchor does not have a navigation target.

questions.question: Which HTML tag inserts a line break inside text?
questions.explanation: The line break element creates a new line without starting a new paragraph.

questions.question: Which semantic tag should wrap the dominant content of a page?
questions.explanation: <main> should contain unique core content, excluding repeated sidebars/nav.

questions.question: Which element is specifically intended to embed video content?
questions.explanation: The <video> element supports native playback controls and source formats.

questions.question: Which tag defines a table row?
questions.explanation: Rows are created using <tr> and contain header/data cells.

questions.question: Which tag is used for a header cell inside a table?
questions.explanation: <th> cells are semantically headers and can improve table accessibility.

questions.question: Which syntax correctly writes a comment in HTML?
questions.explanation: HTML comments start with <!-- and end with -->.

questions.question: Which meta declaration is commonly used for UTF-8 character encoding?
questions.explanation: UTF-8 supports a broad set of characters and is the standard default.

questions.question: Which value opens an anchor in a new tab/window when used with target?
questions.explanation: target="_blank" opens the destination in a new browsing context.

questions.question: Which of the following is a void element in HTML?
questions.explanation: Void elements do not require closing tags (e.g., input, img, br).

questions.question: Which tag conveys strong importance (not just visual bold)?
questions.explanation: <strong> has semantic emphasis, unlike purely visual styling.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 2 - Forms & Validation
sets.description: Form controls, attributes, and validation patterns aligned to common proctored UI MCQ style.

questions.question: If method is omitted in <form>, which HTTP method is used by default?
questions.explanation: Browsers default form submission method to GET if method is not specified.

questions.question: Which input type masks user-entered characters on screen?
questions.explanation: type="password" masks characters for privacy during entry.

questions.question: Which attribute makes a form field mandatory before submission?
questions.explanation: required triggers native browser validation on empty values.

questions.question: Which attribute in <label> should match the target input id?
questions.explanation: for="inputId" binds the label to an input, improving usability/accessibility.

questions.question: How do radio buttons become mutually exclusive in a group?
options: They share the same name attribute.
It is used to control layout behavior.
questions.explanation: Radio inputs with identical name values form one selectable group.

questions.question: Which control allows selecting multiple independent options?
questions.explanation: Checkboxes are independent toggles; radios are single-select in a group.

questions.question: Which HTML element creates a drop-down option list?
questions.explanation: <select> wraps one or more <option> elements for dropdown selection.

questions.question: Which form control is used for multi-line text input?
questions.explanation: <textarea> is designed for longer, multi-line user input.

questions.question: Which attribute shows hint text inside an empty input box?
questions.explanation: Placeholder text appears until the user provides a value.

questions.question: Which input attribute determines the key used in submitted form data?
questions.explanation: Server-side parsers use the input name as the field key.

questions.question: Inside a form, what is the default type of a <button> element?
questions.explanation: Without an explicit type, <button> acts as submit in forms.

questions.question: Which form attribute specifies where submitted data is sent?
questions.explanation: action contains the target URL for form submission.

questions.question: Which attribute can be used to disable browser autofill suggestions?
questions.explanation: Setting autocomplete off requests browsers not to auto-fill fields.

questions.question: Which attribute applies regex-like validation rules to input values?
questions.explanation: pattern enables built-in value matching for specific string formats.

questions.question: Which pair of attributes commonly sets lower and upper numeric limits?
questions.explanation: min/max constrain number, date, and range-type input values.

questions.question: Which element semantically groups related form controls?
questions.explanation: <fieldset> groups controls and often appears with a <legend>.

questions.question: Which element provides a caption/title for a fieldset?
questions.explanation: <legend> describes the purpose of grouped controls.

questions.question: Which element provides predefined suggestions for an <input list="...">?
questions.explanation: datalist offers selectable suggestions while still allowing custom input.

questions.question: Which form attribute disables native browser validation on submit?
questions.explanation: novalidate lets custom JavaScript validation run without browser blocking UI.

questions.question: What happens to a disabled input during form submission?
options: They share the same name attribute.
It is used to control layout behavior.
questions.explanation: Disabled controls are omitted from the submitted payload.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 3 - CSS Core Concepts
sets.description: Selectors, box model, units, and positioning fundamentals for high-accuracy CSS rounds.

questions.question: Which syntax correctly applies a CSS rule?
questions.explanation: CSS rules consist of selector + declaration block.

questions.question: Which rel value is used when linking an external stylesheet?
questions.explanation: link rel="stylesheet" tells the browser to treat the target as CSS.

questions.question: Which selector targets elements having class="card"?
questions.explanation: Class selectors are prefixed with a dot.

questions.question: Which selector targets the element with id="header"?
questions.explanation: ID selectors are prefixed with #.

questions.question: Which selector matches all elements on a page?
questions.explanation: The universal selector applies to every element.

questions.question: What does the selector "div p" target?
options: An ID selector has higher specificity than a class selector.
It starts on a new line and can take full available width.
questions.explanation: A descendant selector matches nested descendants, not only direct children.

questions.question: Which has higher specificity in CSS?
options: An ID selector has higher specificity than a class selector.
It starts on a new line and can take full available width.
questions.explanation: Specificity order favors IDs over classes and elements.

questions.question: Which list correctly represents the CSS box model layers?
options: An ID selector has higher specificity than a class selector.
It clips overflowing content that does not fit the box.
questions.explanation: The box model starts from content and expands outward.

questions.question: Which rule commonly centers a fixed-width block horizontally?
questions.explanation: Auto left/right margins center block-level elements with constrained width.

questions.question: How does display: block behave by default?
options: It starts on a new line and can take full available width.
It allows setting width/height while still flowing inline.
Width and height generally do not apply in the same way as block boxes.
questions.explanation: Block-level boxes break line flow and expand by default.

questions.question: What is true for display: inline elements?
options: It starts on a new line and can take full available width.
It allows setting width/height while still flowing inline.
Width and height generally do not apply in the same way as block boxes.
questions.explanation: Inline boxes flow with text and do not behave like block boxes.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of display: inline-block?
options: It allows setting width/height while still flowing inline.
Width and height generally do not apply in the same way as block boxes.
It starts on a new line and can take full available width.
questions.explanation: inline-block combines inline flow with box dimensions.

questions.question: How does position: relative affect an element?
options: The nearest positioned ancestor (or initial containing block if none).
The element remains in normal flow but can be offset from its original position.
On positioned elements in stacking contexts.
questions.explanation: Relative positioning keeps layout space reserved.

questions.question: position: absolute is positioned relative to what by default?
options: The nearest positioned ancestor (or initial containing block if none).
On positioned elements in stacking contexts.
The element remains in normal flow but can be offset from its original position.
questions.explanation: Absolute elements use the closest ancestor with non-static position.

questions.question: When does z-index generally apply?
options: The element remains in normal flow but can be offset from its original position.
The nearest positioned ancestor (or initial containing block if none).
On positioned elements in stacking contexts.
questions.explanation: z-index influences stacking order when positioning/context rules apply.

questions.question: Which value is a valid 6-digit hex color?
questions.explanation: Hex colors use # followed by 3 or 6 hexadecimal digits.

questions.question: What is rem based on?
options: An ID selector has higher specificity than a class selector.
The current element or parent font size context.
questions.explanation: rem is stable across nesting because it references root font size.

questions.question: What is em based on?
options: The current element or parent font size context.
An ID selector has higher specificity than a class selector.
questions.explanation: em scales relative to surrounding font context and nesting.

questions.question: What does overflow: hidden do when content exceeds box bounds?
options: An ID selector has higher specificity than a class selector.
It clips overflowing content that does not fit the box.
questions.explanation: Overflow hidden removes scrollbars and hides excess content.

questions.question: Which numeric range is valid for opacity?
questions.explanation: 0 is fully transparent and 1 is fully opaque.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 4 - Layout & Responsive Design
sets.description: Flexbox, Grid, media queries, and responsive behavior in practical UI implementation.

questions.question: What is the default value of flex-direction in a flex container?
questions.explanation: Flex items are laid out horizontally by default.

questions.question: Which property aligns flex items along the main axis?
questions.explanation: Main-axis distribution is controlled by justify-content.

questions.question: Which property aligns flex items along the cross axis?
questions.explanation: Cross-axis alignment in flex containers uses align-items.

questions.question: Which flex property allows items to move to the next line?
questions.explanation: flex-wrap: wrap lets overflowing items continue on new rows/columns.

questions.question: Which property adds consistent spacing between flex/grid children?
questions.explanation: gap works in modern flex and grid layouts.

questions.question: Which CSS Grid property defines track columns explicitly?
questions.explanation: Grid columns are declared using grid-template-columns.

questions.question: In CSS Grid, what does 1fr represent?
options: Track size is at least 200px and can grow up to 1fr.
One fraction of the available free space.
questions.explanation: fr units divide leftover space proportionally.

questions.question: What does minmax(200px, 1fr) mean in grid?
options: One fraction of the available free space.
Track size is at least 200px and can grow up to 1fr.
questions.explanation: minmax defines lower and upper bounds for track sizing.

questions.question: In mobile-first CSS, which query is commonly used to add larger-screen styles?
questions.explanation: Mobile-first starts base styles small and enhances with min-width breakpoints.

questions.question: Which meta tag improves responsive sizing on mobile browsers?
questions.explanation: Without proper viewport meta, mobile browsers may scale pages unexpectedly.

questions.question: Which property value keeps an element fixed until a scroll threshold, then pins it?
questions.explanation: Sticky combines relative and fixed behavior based on scroll position.

questions.question: Which property controls how replaced content fills a media box?
questions.explanation: object-fit defines image/video scaling behavior in assigned dimensions.

questions.question: Which function is commonly used to move an element horizontally?
questions.explanation: transform: translateX(...) shifts elements on the X axis.

questions.question: Which property enables smooth change between two CSS states?
questions.explanation: Transitions animate changes in declared properties over time.

questions.question: Which at-rule defines keyframes for CSS animations?
questions.explanation: Keyframes specify animation stages between 0% and 100%.

questions.question: Which CSS function is useful for fluid typography with min and max bounds?
questions.explanation: clamp(min, preferred, max) provides controlled responsive scaling.

questions.question: What does 1vw represent in CSS units?
options: One fraction of the available free space.
Track size is at least 200px and can grow up to 1fr.
questions.explanation: Viewport width units scale with the current browser width.

questions.question: What does 1vh represent in CSS units?
options: Track size is at least 200px and can grow up to 1fr.
One fraction of the available free space.
questions.explanation: Viewport height units scale with the current browser height.

questions.question: Which pseudo-class applies when a pointer hovers over an element?
questions.explanation: :hover triggers style changes during pointer hover.

questions.question: Which pseudo-class is preferred for accessible keyboard focus indication?
questions.explanation: :focus-visible avoids showing focus ring for mouse-only interaction.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 5 - JavaScript Fundamentals
sets.description: Core JavaScript behavior, type system, operators, and control flow essentials.

questions.question: Which declaration has block scope and allows reassignment?
questions.explanation: let is block scoped and mutable.

questions.question: Which declaration creates a block-scoped binding that cannot be reassigned?
questions.explanation: const prevents reassignment of the binding name.

questions.question: Which declaration is function-scoped and hoisted in legacy JavaScript?
questions.explanation: var uses function scope and has historical hoisting behavior.

questions.question: What is the result of typeof null in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: This is a long-standing JavaScript quirk.

questions.question: Which operator checks both value and type equality?
questions.explanation: Strict equality avoids type coercion.

questions.question: Which statement is true about NaN?
options: It falls back only when the left side is null or undefined.
NaN is not equal to itself.
questions.explanation: NaN !== NaN by specification.

questions.question: Which string delimiter supports ${expression} interpolation?
questions.explanation: Template literals use backticks and allow interpolation.

questions.question: What does Number("42") return?
questions.explanation: Number() performs numeric conversion.

questions.question: What is parseInt("08", 10)?
questions.explanation: With radix 10, parseInt parses decimal 08 as 8.

questions.question: What is Boolean("")?
questions.explanation: Empty strings are falsy values.

questions.question: What is Boolean("0")?
questions.explanation: Non-empty strings are truthy, including "0".

questions.question: What does the nullish coalescing operator (??) do?
options: NaN is not equal to itself.
It falls back only when the left side is null or undefined.
questions.explanation: Unlike ||, ?? does not treat 0 or "" as missing.

questions.question: What does optional chaining (?.) return when the base is null/undefined?
options: NaN is not equal to itself.
It falls back only when the left side is null or undefined.
questions.explanation: Optional chaining short-circuits safe property access.

questions.question: What is typeof undefined?
questions.explanation: The typeof operator returns "undefined" for undefined values.

questions.question: What is isNaN("hello")?
questions.explanation: Global isNaN coerces and then tests numeric validity.

questions.question: What is Number.isNaN("hello")?
questions.explanation: Number.isNaN checks without coercion; string is not NaN.

questions.question: What is 0 == false?
questions.explanation: Loose equality performs coercion before comparison.

questions.question: What is 0 === false?
questions.explanation: Strict equality compares type and value.

questions.question: What is [] + [] in JavaScript?
options: NaN is not equal to itself.
It falls back only when the left side is null or undefined.
questions.explanation: Arrays are coerced to strings before concatenation.

questions.question: What is true + true in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: Booleans convert to 1 and 1 in numeric addition.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 6 - Functions, Arrays, and Objects
sets.description: Higher-order methods, object handling, and frequently tested JavaScript utility patterns.

questions.question: What does Array.prototype.map return?
options: A new array containing elements that pass the test
The first matching element, or undefined
A new array with transformed elements
To accumulate array values into a single result
questions.explanation: map never mutates by default and returns a new collection.

questions.question: What does Array.prototype.filter return?
options: A new array containing elements that pass the test
To accumulate array values into a single result
A new array with transformed elements
The first matching element, or undefined
questions.explanation: filter keeps only elements matching the predicate.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Array.prototype.reduce?
options: A new array with transformed elements
The first matching element, or undefined
A new array containing elements that pass the test
To accumulate array values into a single result
questions.explanation: reduce folds values using an accumulator callback.

questions.question: What does Array.prototype.find return?
options: A new array containing elements that pass the test
To accumulate array values into a single result
The first matching element, or undefined
A new array with transformed elements
questions.explanation: find stops at first predicate success.

questions.question: What does Array.prototype.forEach return?
questions.explanation: forEach is for side effects, not transformed return values.

questions.question: What does array.push(value) return?
options: A new array containing elements that pass the test
A new array with transformed elements
To accumulate array values into a single result
questions.explanation: push mutates and returns length.

questions.question: What does array.pop() do?
options: A new array with transformed elements
To accumulate array values into a single result
A new array containing elements that pass the test
Removes and returns the last element
questions.explanation: pop mutates the array from the end.

questions.question: What does array.shift() do?
options: A new array containing elements that pass the test
To accumulate array values into a single result
Removes and returns the first element
A new array with transformed elements
questions.explanation: shift mutates from the beginning and reindexes.

questions.question: What does array.unshift(value) return?
options: To accumulate array values into a single result
The new array length after adding to the front
A new array with transformed elements
A new array containing elements that pass the test
questions.explanation: unshift prepends elements and returns updated length.

questions.question: What does the spread syntax [...arr] create for arrays?
options: A new array with transformed elements
A new array containing elements that pass the test
To accumulate array values into a single result
A shallow copy of the array
questions.explanation: Nested references remain shared because spread is shallow.

questions.question: What does this assignment do: const [a, b] = arr?
options: Array destructuring into variables a and b
A new array containing elements that pass the test
To accumulate array values into a single result
A new array with transformed elements
questions.explanation: Destructuring extracts by index position.

questions.question: Which method returns an array of object own enumerable keys?
questions.explanation: Object.keys returns string keys in an array.

questions.question: Which method checks whether an object has a specific own property?
questions.explanation: hasOwnProperty avoids inherited property confusion.

questions.question: What does JSON.stringify(obj) produce?
options: A new array with transformed elements
A new array containing elements that pass the test
A JavaScript value/object parsed from JSON text
questions.explanation: Stringify serializes plain data into JSON text.

questions.question: What does JSON.parse(jsonText) return?
options: A JavaScript value/object parsed from JSON text
A new array containing elements that pass the test
A new array with transformed elements
questions.explanation: Parse converts JSON string into usable JS data.

questions.question: How does this behave in arrow functions?
options: It usually refers to the object before the dot
Arrow functions do not bind their own this
A function that retains access to outer lexical variables
questions.explanation: Arrow this is lexical (captured from surrounding scope).

questions.question: How is this determined in a regular method call obj.fn()?
options: It usually refers to the object before the dot
A function that retains access to outer lexical variables
Arrow functions do not bind their own this
questions.explanation: Method invocation sets this to the receiver object.

questions.question: What is a closure in JavaScript?
options: It usually refers to the object before the dot
Arrow functions do not bind their own this
A function that retains access to outer lexical variables
questions.explanation: Closures keep scope references after outer execution completes.

questions.question: When are default function parameters applied?
options: A function that retains access to outer lexical variables
It usually refers to the object before the dot
Arrow functions do not bind their own this
questions.explanation: Defaults do not apply for null unless explicitly handled.

questions.question: What does a rest parameter (...args) collect?
options: It usually refers to the object before the dot
Arrow functions do not bind their own this
A function that retains access to outer lexical variables
questions.explanation: Rest parameters gather variadic inputs into one array.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 7 - DOM & Events
sets.description: Document manipulation and event model behavior often tested in practical JavaScript rounds.

questions.question: Which DOM API selects one element by its id value?
questions.explanation: getElementById returns the unique element for a given id, if present.

questions.question: Which method returns the first element that matches a CSS selector?
questions.explanation: querySelector accepts any valid CSS selector and returns first match.

questions.question: Which method returns all matches for a CSS selector?
questions.explanation: querySelectorAll returns a static NodeList of matches.

questions.question: Which property sets or gets only textual content (no HTML parsing)?
questions.explanation: textContent is safer when you only need plain text.

questions.question: Which property parses a string as HTML and injects nodes?
questions.explanation: innerHTML supports markup insertion but needs sanitization for untrusted input.

questions.question: Which method creates a new element node in memory?
questions.explanation: createElement prepares a node before insertion into DOM.

questions.question: Which method appends a node as the last child?
questions.explanation: appendChild inserts nodes at the end of a parent.

questions.question: Which method removes an element from the DOM directly?
questions.explanation: remove deletes the element itself from its parent.

questions.question: Which method registers an event listener on an element?
questions.explanation: Listeners can be attached for click, input, keydown, and more.

questions.question: In an event handler, what does event.target usually represent?
options: The actual element that triggered the event
The element the listener is attached to
Stops the browser default action for that event
Stops the event from bubbling/capturing further
questions.explanation: target is where the event originated.

questions.question: In an event handler, what does event.currentTarget represent?
options: The actual element that triggered the event
Stops the browser default action for that event
Stops the event from bubbling/capturing further
The element the listener is attached to
questions.explanation: currentTarget can differ from target due to bubbling/capturing.

questions.question: What does event.preventDefault() do?
options: Stops the browser default action for that event
The element the listener is attached to
Stops the event from bubbling/capturing further
The actual element that triggered the event
questions.explanation: Example: prevent link navigation or form submission default behavior.

questions.question: What does event.stopPropagation() do?
options: The actual element that triggered the event
Stops the event from bubbling/capturing further
Stops the browser default action for that event
The element the listener is attached to
questions.explanation: It prevents propagation to ancestors in current event flow.

questions.question: When does DOMContentLoaded fire?
options: The element the listener is attached to
The actual element that triggered the event
After initial HTML is parsed (before all assets necessarily load)
Stops the browser default action for that event
questions.explanation: DOMContentLoaded is ideal for DOM setup without waiting for images.

questions.question: Which API adds a CSS class to an element in a semantic way?
questions.explanation: classList avoids manual string parsing in className.

questions.question: Which API toggles a class on/off?
questions.explanation: toggle is useful for interactive states such as menus/modals.

questions.question: Which method finds the nearest ancestor matching a selector?
questions.explanation: closest traverses upward including the element itself.

questions.question: How are custom data-* attributes commonly accessed in JS?
options: The element the listener is attached to
The actual element that triggered the event
Stops the browser default action for that event
questions.explanation: data-user-id maps to dataset.userId.

questions.question: Which method sets or updates an attribute value on an element?
questions.explanation: setAttribute(name, value) writes attribute data.

questions.question: Which method removes an attribute from an element?
questions.explanation: removeAttribute(name) deletes an existing attribute.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 8 - Browser APIs & Async JS
sets.description: Storage, fetch, promises, async/await, and browser utility APIs for real app scenarios.

questions.question: How long does localStorage data persist by default?
options: A Promise that resolves to a Response object
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: localStorage survives tab and browser restarts for same origin.

questions.question: How long does sessionStorage data persist?
options: For the lifetime of the current tab/session
A Promise that resolves to a Response object
questions.explanation: sessionStorage is scoped to a browsing session/tab.

questions.question: What data type does localStorage store natively?
questions.explanation: Complex values must be serialized to strings first.

questions.question: What should you do before saving an object to localStorage?
options: For the lifetime of the current tab/session
A Promise that resolves to a Response object
questions.explanation: Stringify serializes object data for storage.

questions.question: What does fetch() return immediately?
options: For the lifetime of the current tab/session
A Promise resolving to parsed JSON data
A Promise that resolves to a Response object
questions.explanation: fetch is asynchronous and returns a promise.

questions.question: What does response.json() return?
options: A Promise resolving to parsed JSON data
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
A Promise that resolves to a Response object
questions.explanation: json() itself is asynchronous.

questions.question: What is always returned by an async function?
questions.explanation: Async functions wrap return values in resolved promises.

questions.question: Where can await be used directly?
options: Inside an async function (or supported module top-level contexts)
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: Classic function bodies need async keyword for await.

questions.question: How do you catch errors from an awaited promise?
options: Inside an async function (or supported module top-level contexts)
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: Rejected promises throw inside async functions.

questions.question: What does setTimeout do?
options: For the lifetime of the current tab/session
Runs a callback once after a delay
Runs a callback repeatedly at a time interval
questions.explanation: setTimeout schedules one-time deferred execution.

questions.question: What does setInterval do?
options: Runs a callback repeatedly at a time interval
Runs a callback once after a delay
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: Intervals continue until cleared.

questions.question: How do you stop a running interval?
questions.explanation: clearInterval cancels future interval ticks.

questions.question: Which API helps read query parameters like ?page=2 from a URL?
questions.explanation: URLSearchParams offers easy get/set/iterate for query strings.

questions.question: What does navigator.onLine indicate?
options: A basic online/offline network connectivity hint
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: It is a heuristic flag and not perfect network guarantee.

questions.question: What does history.back() do?
options: Navigates to the previous browser history entry
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: Equivalent to clicking browser back in many contexts.

questions.question: What does location.reload() do?
options: For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: The browser re-requests or reloads current URL resources.

questions.question: How are cookies exposed in JavaScript on document?
options: As a semicolon-separated string in document.cookie
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: document.cookie reads/writes cookie text format.

questions.question: Which function safely encodes text for use in query parameter values?
questions.explanation: It escapes reserved/special URL characters in components.

questions.question: How does Promise.all behave if one input promise rejects?
options: It settles as soon as the first promise settles
It rejects immediately with that rejection reason
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
questions.explanation: Promise.all requires all promises to fulfill.

questions.question: How does Promise.race settle?
options: It settles as soon as the first promise settles
For the lifetime of the current tab/session
It rejects immediately with that rejection reason
questions.explanation: Race returns the first fulfilled or rejected outcome.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 9 - Mixed Output & Logic
sets.description: Output-focused MCQs in the style of placement/proctored rounds with deterministic answers.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(1 + "2") ?
questions.explanation: Number + string triggers string concatenation.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log("5" - 2) ?
questions.explanation: The - operator coerces operands to numbers.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(typeof NaN) ?
questions.explanation: NaN is a special numeric value.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(Boolean([])) ?
questions.explanation: Any object, including arrays, is truthy.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log([] == false) ?
questions.explanation: Loose equality coercion makes this expression true.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log([1,2,3].length) ?
questions.explanation: length returns the number of array slots.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log("abc".toUpperCase()) ?
questions.explanation: toUpperCase converts letters to uppercase.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(Math.max(4, 9, 1)) ?
questions.explanation: Math.max returns largest argument.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(Math.floor(4.9)) ?
questions.explanation: Math.floor truncates down to nearest integer.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log("5" + 1) ?
questions.explanation: String concatenation occurs with + and a string operand.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(2 ** 3) ?
questions.explanation: ** is exponentiation in JavaScript.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(10 % 3) ?
questions.explanation: % returns remainder after division.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log([1,2].concat([3]).join("-")) ?
questions.explanation: concat merges arrays, join builds a hyphen-delimited string.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log("hello".includes("ell")) ?
questions.explanation: includes checks if a substring exists.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(typeof []) ?
questions.explanation: Arrays are specialized objects in JS.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(null == undefined) ?
questions.explanation: Loose equality treats null and undefined as equal.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(null === undefined) ?
questions.explanation: Strict equality requires identical type and value.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log([..."hi"].length) ?
questions.explanation: Spreading string into array yields one item per character.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(parseInt("101", 2)) ?
questions.explanation: Binary 101 equals decimal 5.

questions.question: What is logged by: console.log(Number.isInteger(4.0)) ?
questions.explanation: 4.0 is numerically an integer value.

sets.title: TCS UI Set 10 - Web Best Practices
sets.description: Security, performance, and accessibility concepts useful in practical UI interviews and assessments.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of using HTTPS instead of HTTP?
options: Execution of unauthorized scripts and injected content
Escape or sanitize untrusted input before insertion.
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
To prevent reverse-tabnabbing and isolate window context
questions.explanation: HTTPS protects confidentiality and integrity during network transfer.

questions.question: What is a primary frontend defense against XSS when rendering user data?
options: It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
Escape or sanitize untrusted input before insertion.
To prevent reverse-tabnabbing and isolate window context
Execution of unauthorized scripts and injected content
questions.explanation: Untrusted HTML/JS must never be injected unsafely.

questions.question: What does a Content Security Policy (CSP) help mitigate?
options: Execution of unauthorized scripts and injected content
Escape or sanitize untrusted input before insertion.
To prevent reverse-tabnabbing and isolate window context
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
questions.explanation: CSP restricts trusted resource origins and script behavior.

questions.question: Why add rel="noopener noreferrer" to target="_blank" links?
options: It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
Escape or sanitize untrusted input before insertion.
Execution of unauthorized scripts and injected content
To prevent reverse-tabnabbing and isolate window context
questions.explanation: noopener breaks window.opener linkage to improve security.

questions.question: Why use semantic HTML elements instead of generic divs everywhere?
options: They improve accessibility and document meaning.
A concise description of the image meaning/function
Every control should have an associated label.
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
questions.explanation: Semantic structure helps screen readers and maintainability.

questions.question: For informative images, what should alt text provide?
options: It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
A concise description of the image meaning/function
They improve accessibility and document meaning.
Every control should have an associated label.
questions.explanation: Alt should communicate useful content, not file names.

questions.question: What is a key accessibility rule for form controls?
options: A concise description of the image meaning/function
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
Every control should have an associated label.
They improve accessibility and document meaning.
questions.explanation: Labels improve usability for all users and assistive tech.

questions.question: Which ARIA feature helps announce dynamic updates to assistive tech?
questions.explanation: aria-live regions announce changing content updates.

questions.question: How can you reduce initial network payload size for faster load?
options: It delays script execution until after HTML parsing.
External files improve caching and policy control.
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
questions.explanation: Minification and compression reduce transfer bytes.

questions.question: Which image attribute defers offscreen image loading?
questions.explanation: Lazy loading postpones non-critical image fetches.

questions.question: How does using defer on script tags help page rendering?
options: It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
External files improve caching and policy control.
It delays script execution until after HTML parsing.
questions.explanation: defer prevents parser blocking for external scripts.

questions.question: Why avoid large inline scripts/styles in production pages?
options: External files improve caching and policy control.
It delays script execution until after HTML parsing.
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
questions.explanation: External assets are cacheable and easier to secure/manage.

questions.question: Which browser API is preferred for smooth animation loops?
questions.explanation: requestAnimationFrame syncs with repaint cycles for efficiency.

questions.question: Which technique limits rapid function calls until user stops typing?
questions.explanation: Debounce delays execution until activity settles.

questions.question: Which technique guarantees execution at most once per interval during frequent events?
questions.explanation: Throttle caps call frequency during bursts like scrolling.

questions.question: What is a common advantage of service workers in web apps?
options: Escape or sanitize untrusted input before insertion.
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
Offline caching and network interception capabilities
Execution of unauthorized scripts and injected content
questions.explanation: Service workers can cache assets and serve offline experiences.

questions.question: Where is it generally safer to keep highly sensitive session tokens?
options: It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
HttpOnly secure cookies managed by the server
Execution of unauthorized scripts and injected content
Escape or sanitize untrusted input before insertion.
questions.explanation: HttpOnly cookies are not readable by JavaScript, reducing XSS exposure.

questions.question: What does the same-origin policy primarily restrict?
options: Cross-origin read access to protected resources
Escape or sanitize untrusted input before insertion.
Execution of unauthorized scripts and injected content
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
questions.explanation: SOP prevents arbitrary reading across different origins.

questions.question: Which response header controls allowed cross-origin request origins?
questions.explanation: CORS policies are communicated using this header.

questions.question: Which approach helps maintain positive UX during async operations?
options: Execution of unauthorized scripts and injected content
Show clear loading and success/error feedback states
It encrypts data in transit between client and server.
Escape or sanitize untrusted input before insertion.
questions.explanation: Feedback reduces user confusion and improves trust.
